speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 12th of July, 1996, and we are in Sydney, Australia. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, during that time when you are in Bussum, did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bussum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bussum. Did you find out anything about your baby or what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know where he was. But where he was staying was very close to the main highway. And then he was about two or something. Then that man came to me where he was staying. So it first [INAUDIBLE] a father. And he said, you have to take Paul back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, did you try and find him at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I, at one stage, I was so desperate. I want to see my baby. And I didn't have exactly an idea where he was, approximately. And I went on a train in Amsterdam. And the place where those people lived, you had to go by tram, train and tram. And I came there, but there was a curfew in Holland. At 8 o'clock you had to be inside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I came in, in Hilversum, which was the place I had to get off the train. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the train didn't go any more. So I went to a stationmaster and said, can I hire a bike here? No. I said, can I get a taxi? No. So I went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Then I meet in the train people I've never seen in my life. And I said to them, I haven't got accommodation here, and I don't know where to stay overnight. So they said, you come with us and stay the night with us, which I did. I didn't say who I was or what, nothing. So they took me home. And people were very helpful in Holland. And then the next day, I went back again to the station. And you had to walk downstairs to go to the tram. And everybody was running for the tram, and so did I. And while I walked those steps down, somebody grabbed me my neck and said, you come with us. I didn't have a clue what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I thought, I'm caught, false papers with me, of course, OK two men. Did you-- have you been here last night? I said no. You are sure you didn't come here last night? I said, yes, I'm sure. Oh, I think you were here. I said, yes, I was here. Why did you say no? I said, it's such a shock when you grabbed me that for the first instance, I thought it's better to say no. Mm. What were you doing here? I said I was going to visit some friends. That didn't ask the address, luckily. I didn't know the address, anyway. Oh, did you try to hire a bike? I said yes. Why? I said because the tram didn't go anymore. And they asked me thousand and one questions. And then it started to get dark. So I thought, I'll do one big spoon, and I said, if you don't let me go now, I miss the train to Amsterdam because I have to be in by 8 o'clock. So they just talk with each other and then let me go. But I was so nervous. And I came in Amsterdam. I took the wrong tram to go to the people where I was staying. But anyway, I was inside in time. So that was that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, at one stage, when I went for that food business, then we came to a bridge, the [INAUDIBLE] Bridge was the name. And nobody was allowed over the bridge. So we came at the bridge, and there were people standing there who lived there. And they were from the railway. And they were on strike in protest to the German.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they said to us, you can't cross the bridge. And the men, special not, because they pick him up straightaway. So you better come with us. And we stayed the night with them. And the next morning, they gave him warm clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went over the bridge because in that place over the bridge, I had an uncle hiding with his wife and two children. And I had false identification papers sewn in my underwear for them. And I had to bring them money from my father. So I had to get over that bridge. So anyway, we went over the bridge all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your uncle's name that was in hiding there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was the-- when you met him, how did he interact with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was fantastic. But from that whole family, is one daughter still alive. The rest is all gone. So-- and that's how it went on with the whole family, with ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, can we just go back a little bit? When your-- when your mother was rounded up--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in August.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what that meant when she didn't come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: That she never come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I realized that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't know exactly what they did. But we knew they would never come back. We were told they go to working camps. But that was, of course, [GERMAN]. So that was not true at all. And the transport my mother went with was not big enough. And then filled it up with nuns and priest, which were Jewish in chains to try to save their lives. But they didn't. And the same was if you had four-- you had to have four Jewish grandparents, then you were declared Jewish. If you had less, you had a chance. They let you go. So they worked it all out. It's unbelievable what they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, in Bussum, how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bussum? That was actually the last stop I was. And I was there, oh, maybe year, maybe a bit more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you-- were you ever suspected of being Jewish in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I told you, we-- we had fires because, you know, Holland's very cold. And we had to have wood to-- we had a big stove in the kitchen. And we had to have wood. And we pinched under the ice from the German a tree and cut the tree in pieces on the street. And then we schlepped all that inside to get a bit of-- of heating for cooking and-- and-- and-- warm, and all that business. And in the bathroom upstairs, we had water because sometimes, you were without water. And we had kept that water in case of emergency. And when my son came back, I had to ask permission, of course, from that woman if I could bring my son in. But he was so blond. And he didn't look Jewish at all. And-- and then he was very difficult, very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how you got him back, what the process was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they just bought him back, that's all, because they were scared. They lived on the main road, on [INAUDIBLE] Road.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the family's name that had him?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. And when-- he went to them when he was six days old. And then they put him in like the [INAUDIBLE]. They got a paper to prove that-- that he was registered by the council as being born to them, because that was the safest. And later, they had a daughter who looked a bit like him. It was like twins, also very blonde, and chubby, and all that. But he was very difficult. And then at one stage, when he was back only a short while, but I was a nervous wreck in those days, as you can imagine. I lost my husband. I lost my mother, my whole family. And then when he had to eat, he starts screaming. But when you see him after, you wouldn't believe it, because he is very, very big. But anyway, in those days, he had-- when he came to those people, he had gastric. And he didn't want food. And he didn't want to go on the potty. He didn't want anything. and. I was such a nervous wreck that one day, I took him upstairs and put him with his head down in the bathtub where the water was [LAUGHS] to keep him quiet. Oh, it was terrible. It was really terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay the Dutch family to take him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those people, I didn't pay. No, I worked there. No, I didn't pay. But the other people I did, yeah. There were a lot of people who didn't take money, or did it just to help people. But like that retired policeman, he had three sons. And the youngest was still at home. And the middle one was hiding with his parents because he had to go to Germany to work. And a lot of people didn't want that. The eldest was married. That was the one who picked me up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then at night when we had dinner, because we gave so much money, the-- they had to bring food on the table. And then that young guy used to say, ooh, was a king's meal again tonight. And we laughed about it because we knew exactly what was coming, you know? So-- but that man was horrible. She was OK, but, well, horrible. Nah. Did you stay in Bussum till the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then we had an enormous trip to Holland, to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody came with a bike, a pushbike and a platform. I don't know how you call that here. The front had a platform. They don't know those bikes here. And I was sitting on it, and my son in an trolley on it, and my stepmother, and my father. And that's how we drove back to Amsterdam. And then we came at a big bridge in Holland, and the Canadians were there because of all Canadians who were coming in Holland then. And we stopped, and we asked for bread for the baby. They had white bread, whiter than white, and cigarettes for my father. And then we rented in a couple of rooms. We were in the-- my father and I went to Amsterdam before and rented a couple of rooms. And there we lived, these-- the four of us. Yeah, he and his wife and Paul and me, yeah, that's it. And then we got through the government a house where the-- we had also a Nazi business in Holland. They called it the NSB, NSB. And those people lived in those houses had to get out. And those were distributed to people who came back from the war. And I lived there with my father and his wife. And my father had a child with that woman. And then we didn't know where the child was. And we eventually found her. And so she came to live there too. And I didn't know who was back or who was not. And in those days, there was a dance hall in Amsterdam. And people went there to see if they found relatives or friends or whatever. But I never wanted to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a cousin who went there. And then we had devaluation from the money. And the people from the dance hall were very clever, and they gave coupons. Until a certain date, you could hand it in for drinks or whatever. And I got a phone call one night from a cousin of mine. And he said, look, I'm going there. Would you like to come? And I said no. I'm not interested.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father said, you complain all the time. You don't go anywhere. You go and Paul-- that is my son-- stay here. So I went. So I came there, and it was like sardines. You have no idea. People were dancing. There was music. And it was good atmosphere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the waiter said, how many people? I said two. He said, I'll have a look if I find any chairs for you. I said, OK. So he went to the back, came with two chairs. I walked around and put them down, and I was sitting next to my husband. Which husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My second husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't know I was alive, and I didn't know he was alive. Philip Abram. And then they-- we talked, of course. And he walked me home. And I think till 3 o'clock in the morning we were talking and talking. And when he came back, he was very sick. He came back in the Nazi uniform and Nazi butes-- boots. And he was a big man. And he-- his weight was, I think, 5 and 1/2 stone. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had he been?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz. And if the war would have gone on two days long, he wouldn't have come back. He was in a way very lucky because of the name Abram, everything went on alphabet. So he was the first one to be picked up. Then he was brought to the camp where I told you where the German Jews came in, Westerbork. And he worked there. He had a little house there. He worked for the military police. He repaired cars. He could do anything. And then after a while, when they were certain he was trustworthy, they gave him papers to go in and out of camp. It's a big truck to get food and all that business. And they observed him for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he went to another little town close by. And that man, he became friendly with. And after a long time, he said to him, listen, Philip, would you be interested to work for the underground movement? And he said yes. So he gave his papers to copy. And he smuggled people out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then somebody, but we don't know who-- we knew who, but doesn't matter. Then somebody sent the letter to the camp and said, if you need any help, ask for Phillip Abram. And if that wouldn't have happened, he would have never gone to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the German called him straight away because they intercepted that letter and asked him what it was all about. And he denied, of course, ever seeing. But straightway, he had to go to Auschwitz. And then the military police came and said to him, take the car and go. But then he had to leave his wife behind, and he didn't want that. So they went, because in no time, everybody knew he was caught, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And before that, somebody whispered that he had a son. It was Karel. And then he was called up. And they said, I'll give you a week to find your son. Well, he never found him. First of all, he didn't know where he was. And second, he never found him, of course. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was his son?

speaker: interviewee
text: One and half when he went hiding, about two, maybe. [DRINKS] And then the war is over, and he came back. And then he--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife was gassed straightaway when she came in Auschwitz. Straightaway, she went to the gas chamber. And then-- she was 24 years old. And then [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he was looking for his son, for Karel. But nobody knows anything. At one stage, they put up lists on certain shops and then you could look for names. And if you were lucky, you found somebody. But that was like a miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, then he found out on the top of Holland was an organization who registered all those kids who were hiding there. So he could hardly walk because he was very sick. After he came back, he couldn't keep any food in him. So he was more in bed rest that he was up. And he stayed with his sister then for a while. So he went with the friend to the top of Holland and wanted to go to that office. And when he walked there, a man came out of a door. And he said, were you looking for? And he said, well, I look for my son because I think my son must be here somewhere. And he said, what is his name? He said, Karel Abram. So he said, he got a birthmark on his leg. So the man said, you don't have to look any further. He's in my place. And my husband bust out. That was a miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before that, he was in another place. But those people were also scared something happened, so he had to move to those people. And there he stayed till after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he find Karel before you saw him in that dance hall or after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. But he took him to Amsterdam. But because, as I said, he was sick all the time, he couldn't look after him. So he had to bring him back to those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, I don't know. Can't remember. No, I can't remember. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who looked after him when he brought him home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the parents, and they had three grown-up children, two boys, who is a minister, one of them, and a-- and a daughter, who was then still at home. And I can't remember that name. Anyway, it was in Hovart, very little village. And so when I came on the scene, we went there every few weeks to those people. And we stayed overnight because it was, in those days, a long trip, two to three hours drive, and until we got to our own place.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, then I was still living with my father. And then we took him. And he couldn't speak a word of Dutch. He spoke dialect, and that was terrible. And my husband had to go to work. He start to work with my father. And then the child said something to me. And maybe three times I said, I beg your pardon? I couldn't follow what he said. It is impossible to understand. So after a few weeks, I said he has to go to school. I can't take it anymore, because he had to wait till his father came home, who had a little bit of feeling for that language. But I didn't at all. So we put him to kinder. And then I-- How old was he when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was then, oh, maybe four and a half, five. And he really thought that I was his mother because she was a similar type. And, for instance, I had a suit. And he found buttons in a-- in a box. And that is, oh, that's from mommy's suit. And I said, yes, because we thought he was too small to tell the story. We-- I didn't believe in that. Now I look at it different, but in those days. And then he visited a relative. And her mother was there. And then the two of them started to talk about the children. Yes, Lien got three different kids. She got one her own, one from Philip, and one together, which is true.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he came home and confronted us. And we were so upset, but so upset. You have no idea, because we thought he was too young to-- to work that out, you know? But that was the truth. It was like that. What could you do? When was Phillip born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Philip was born in '46, October of '46. He will be 50 this year, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was he called Philip?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was called Philip after my father. And my grandson is called Philip after my husband. So we've got three Philips in the family, very confusing. We have now two left. But well, can't help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell Tell me, Selien, how long did you stay in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, actually, I wanted to go straight away. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I had enough on everything what I went through when I couldn't take it anymore. And I said I want to get away. And we applied for America to immigrate there. But you had to have a sponsor, which we didn't have. And it went on a quota system. And then my husband's youngest brother, who came back from the camp, he married and went to Australia with his wife. And then we decided we go to Australia too. And that's how we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was difficult because first of all, I had three kids. And I decided, which was stupid, after all, that we pay the fares. And then everybody in Holland said, you're crazy, because you can go for next to nothing. So-- but I told the lady, the government, that we pay our own fare.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I went there and tell them I couldn't pay for it, I had to have an excuse. And I had an excuse because the house where we were living, we paid key money for. That was in those days. And when we were go to leave, we asked the estate agent if we could do it over to somebody else. And he said yes. So we counted on that money.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then he realized then he get nothing. So he said no. I'll fix it myself. So I didn't have that money. So that was the excuse. And that I told them that. It was the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then they-- we still had to pay, because I found the other day the papers. We still paid 6,000 guilders, which was a lot of money in those days, for four people. But that was that. We'll stop here.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. OK. This is the end of tape 3 of the interview with Selien Abram. It's the 12th of July, 1996. And we are in Sydney, Australia.
